turns.en: Hi everybody, welcome to In Good Company. Today, really exciting, we have Dario Amodei, the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, visiting. Dario is a superstar in the AI world, and together with his team, has developed the Claude language model, one of the best out there. They are backed by Amazon and Google. You are a leading figure on AI safety and ethics, and you even interrupted your holiday to come here and talk to us. So big thanks for coming.
turns.zh: 大家好，欢迎来到《In Good Company》。今天非常激动，我们请到了Anthropic的CEO兼联合创始人Dario Amodei。Dario是AI界的巨星，他和团队开发了Claude语言模型，这是目前最顶尖的模型之一，并且得到了亚马逊和谷歌的支持。你是AI安全与伦理领域的领军人物，甚至中断了假期来和我们交流。非常感谢你的到来。

turns.en: Thank you for having me.
turns.zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

turns.en: Now, what are the latest breakthroughs in AI?
turns.zh: 那么，AI领域最新的突破是什么？

turns.en: Yes, so a few things I could talk about. One is, I think the scaling trends of AI are continuing. So I think we're going to see over the next year much bigger and more powerful models that are able to do greater tasks. In fact, by the time this podcast airs, a new model will be out from Anthropic that will probably be the most intelligent and powerful model in the world. But one area I'm particularly excited about that we're developing in parallel with that is interpretability of models—the ability to see inside our AI models and see why they make the decisions they make. That area has been mainly a research area for the last few years, and it's just at the beginning of starting to have practical applications. So that's one area I'm very excited about.
turns.zh: 是的，我可以谈几点。首先，我认为AI的Scaling（规模扩张）趋势仍在继续。未来一年，我们会看到更大、更强大的模型，能够完成更复杂的任务。实际上，当这期播客播出时，Anthropic将发布一个新模型，它很可能是世界上最智能、最强大的模型。但我特别兴奋的一个领域是模型的可解释性——能够观察AI模型内部，理解它们做出决策的原因。过去几年，这主要是一个研究领域，现在才刚刚开始有实际应用。所以这是我很兴奋的一个方向。

turns.en: Why is that so important?
turns.zh: 为什么这如此重要？

turns.en: If you look at what AI models do today, often you won't understand why an AI model does what it does. I was just talking to someone at lunch. Let's say you want an AI model to be trained on some data to predict what happened with a particular set of financial data. One problem is that if you train it on data from the past, the model might have memorized it because it basically knows what happens—it knows the future. In that case, interpretability might allow you to tell the difference: is the model deducing the answer or memorizing it? Similarly, if a model acts in a way that shows prejudice against a particular group, or appears to do so, can we look at the reasoning of the model? Is it really being driven by prejudice? There are also a number of legal requirements, right? In the EU, there's a right to explanation. So interpretability—being able to see inside the model—could help us understand why models do and say the things they do, and even to intervene and change what they do and say.
turns.zh: 看看今天AI模型的表现，你往往无法理解它们为什么会这样做。我午餐时刚和一个人聊过。假设你想让一个AI模型基于某些数据来预测特定财务数据的结果。一个问题是，如果你用过去的数据训练它，模型可能已经记住了答案，因为它基本知道发生了什么——它知道未来。在这种情况下，可解释性可能让你区分：模型是在推导答案，还是在记忆答案？同样，如果模型表现出对某个群体的偏见，或者看起来如此，我们能检查模型的推理过程吗？它真的是由偏见驱动的吗？此外，还有一些法律要求，对吧？在欧盟，有“解释权”。所以可解释性——能够观察模型内部——可以帮助我们理解模型为什么做和说这些事情，甚至进行干预，改变它们的行为和言论。

turns.en: So a while back, you stated that we still don't know how advanced AI models work. Does this mean that interpretability will solve this problem?
turns.zh: 之前你曾说过，我们仍然不知道先进的AI模型是如何工作的。这是否意味着可解释性会解决这个问题？

turns.en: I wouldn't say solve. I would say we're at the beginning. Maybe we now understand 3% of how they work, really. We're at the level where we can look inside the model and find features that correspond to very complex concepts. One feature might represent the concept of honesty, hesitating, a particular genre of music, a particular type of metaphorical situation a character could be in, or the idea of prejudice for or against various groups. So we have all these features, but we think we've only found a small fraction of what there is. And what we still don't understand is how all these things interact to give us the behaviors we see in models every day. It's a little like the brain, right? We can do brain scans, we can say a little about the human brain, but we don't have a spec sheet for it. We can't go and say, 'This is why that person did exactly what they did.' So will we ever understand fully how they work? I don't know about fully down to the last detail, but I think progress is happening fast, and I'm optimistic about getting there.
turns.zh: 我不会说“解决”。我认为我们才刚刚开始。也许我们现在只理解了它们工作方式的3%。我们能够观察模型内部，找到对应非常复杂概念的特征。一个特征可能代表诚实、犹豫、某种音乐流派、角色所处的某种隐喻情境，或者对某些群体的偏见概念。所以我们有所有这些特征，但我们认为只找到了其中一小部分。我们仍然不理解的是，所有这些特征如何相互作用，产生我们每天看到的模型行为。这有点像大脑，对吧？我们可以做脑部扫描，对人脑有一些了解，但我们没有它的规格说明书。我们不能说，‘这就是那个人为什么那样做的原因。’所以我们能完全理解它们如何工作吗？我不知道是否能细致到最后一个细节，但我认为进展很快，我对达到这个目标持乐观态度。

turns.en: But is progress happening faster than the complexity of the new models?
turns.zh: 但进展的速度是否快于新模型的复杂度？

turns.en: That is a great question, and that is the thing we are contending with. So we are putting a lot of resources behind interpretability of language models to try and keep pace with the rate at which the complexity of the models is increasing. I think this is one of the biggest challenges in the field. The field is moving so fast, including by our own efforts, that we want to make sure our understanding keeps pace with our abilities to produce powerful models.
turns.zh: 这是个好问题，也是我们正在应对的挑战。我们正在投入大量资源用于语言模型的可解释性，试图跟上模型复杂度增长的速度。我认为这是该领域最大的挑战之一。这个领域发展如此之快，包括我们自己的努力，我们希望确保我们的理解能够跟上我们生产强大模型的能力。

turns.en: What's so good about your model?
turns.zh: 你们的模型好在哪里？

turns.en: So this is the Claude model, right? To give some context, we recently released a set of Claude 3 models: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. They are different trade-offs between power and intelligence, and speed and low cost while still being intelligent. At the time Opus was released, it was actually the best all-around model in the world. But I think one thing that particularly made it good is that we put a lot of engineering into its character. We recently put out a post about how we design Claude's character. People have generally found the Claude models are warmer, more human; they enjoy interacting with them more. Some of the other models sound more robotic, more uninspired. We're continuing to innovate quickly, and as I said, by the time this podcast comes out, we'll probably have at least part of a new generation of models out.
turns.zh: 这是Claude模型，对吧？简单介绍一下，我们最近发布了一系列Claude 3模型：Opus、Sonnet和Haiku。它们在能力与智能、速度与低成本之间做了不同的权衡，同时保持了智能水平。Opus发布时，它实际上是世界上最好的全能模型。但我认为它特别好的一个原因是我们在其性格上投入了大量工程。我们最近发布了一篇文章，介绍我们如何设计Claude的性格。人们普遍觉得Claude模型更温暖、更人性化，他们更喜欢与它互动。其他一些模型听起来更机械、更缺乏灵感。我们正在快速创新，正如我所说，当这期播客播出时，我们很可能已经推出了至少部分新一代模型。

turns.en: Tell me about the new one.
turns.zh: 跟我说说新模型吧。

turns.en: I can't say too much about it, but if I had to say a bit, I would say that we're pushing the frontier right now. There's a trade-off between speed, low cost, and quality. You can imagine that as a trade-off curve, a frontier. There's going to be a new generation of models that pushes that frontier outward. So by the time this podcast is out, we'll have a name for it, at least for some of those models. And we'll see that things that you needed the most powerful model to do, you'll be able to do with some of the mid-tier or low-tier models that are faster, cheaper, and even more capable than the previous generation.
turns.zh: 我不能透露太多，但如果非要说一点，那就是我们正在推动前沿。速度、低成本和质量之间存在权衡。你可以把它想象成一条权衡曲线，一个前沿。新一代模型将把这个前沿向外推。所以当这期播客播出时，我们会有一个名字，至少是其中一些模型的名字。我们会看到，以前需要最强大模型才能完成的任务，现在可以用一些中端或低端模型来完成，它们更快、更便宜，甚至比上一代更有能力。

turns.en: So Dario, what's going to be the wow factor here? When I get this model, what is it going to do to me?
turns.zh: 那么Dario，这里的“哇”因素是什么？当我拿到这个模型时，它会给我带来什么？

turns.en: You're going to see models that are better at things like code, math, reasoning. One of my favorites is biology and medicine—that's one of the sets of applications I'm most excited about for the new models. The models we have today are kind of like early undergrads in their knowledge of many things, or like interns. I think we're starting to push that boundary towards advanced undergrads or even beyond.
turns.zh: 你会看到模型在代码、数学、推理等方面表现更好。我最喜欢的一个领域是生物学和医学——这是我对新模型最兴奋的应用方向之一。我们今天的模型在很多知识方面有点像大学低年级学生，或者像实习生。我认为我们正在开始把这个边界推向高年级本科生甚至更高。

turns.en: Graduate level knowledge and so when we think of use of models for drug development or you know in your own industry use of the models for thinking about investing or even trading, I think the models are just going to get a good deal more sophisticated at those tasks. And we're hoping that every few months we can release a new model that pushes those boundaries further and further. Now one of the things which has accelerated lately is just how we weave AI into everything we do. Yes, and with the recent announcement from Apple and OpenAI, how do you look at this?
turns.zh: 研究生级别的知识，所以当我们考虑模型用于药物开发，或者在你自己的行业中用于投资甚至交易时，我认为模型在这些任务上会变得更加复杂。我们希望每隔几个月就能发布一个新模型，不断突破这些边界。最近加速的一件事就是我们如何将AI融入我们所做的一切。是的，随着苹果和OpenAI最近的公告，你怎么看这个？

turns.en: Anthropic thinks of itself more as providing services to enterprises than on the consumer side. So we're thinking a lot about how to integrate AI in work settings. If you think about today's models, today's chatbots, it's a bit like if I took some random person on the street who was very smart but knew nothing about your company, and I brought them in and asked them for advice. What I really like is someone that acts more like an AI model that acts more like someone that's been trained with knowledge of your company for many years. So we're working on connecting our AI models to knowledge databases, having them cite work, be able to use internal enterprise tools, and really integrate with the enterprise as a sort of virtual assistant to an employee. So that's one way I think about driving the integration.
turns.zh: Anthropic 更倾向于将自己定位为向企业提供服务，而不是面向消费者。所以我们正在思考如何将AI融入工作环境。如果你想想现在的模型和聊天机器人，这有点像我从街上随便拉一个很聪明但对你的公司一无所知的人，然后请他给建议。我真正想要的是一个更像AI模型的东西，它就像一个经过多年公司知识训练的人。所以我们正在努力将我们的AI模型连接到知识数据库，让它们能够引用工作内容，使用企业内部工具，并真正作为员工的虚拟助手融入企业。这就是我思考推动整合的一种方式。

ts.sec: Long-term Goal and Race to the Top

turns.en: If you look at the long-term goal of Anthropic, what is the long-term goal?
turns.zh: 如果你看Anthropic的长期目标，那是什么？

turns.en: We're only three and a half years old, by far the newest player in the space that's been able to build models on the frontier. We're a public benefit corporation, and I think our long-term goal is to make sure all of this goes well. That's being done through the vehicle of a company, but if you think about our long-term strategy, what we're really trying to do is create what we call a race to the top. Race to the bottom is a well-known thing where everyone fights to cut corners because market competition is so intense. We think there's a way to have the reverse effect: if you're able to produce higher standards, innovate in ways that make the technology more ethical, then others will follow suit. They'll either be inspired by it or bullied into it by their own employees, public sentiment, or ultimately the law will go in that direction. So we're hoping to provide an example of how to do AI right and pull the rest of the industry along with us. That's a lot of the work behind our interpretability work, safety work, and how we think about responsible scaling. We have something called a responsible scaling policy. So I think our overall goal is to try to help the whole industry be better.
turns.zh: 我们只有三年半的历史，是迄今为止在这个领域能够构建前沿模型的最新玩家。我们是一家公益公司，我认为我们的长期目标是确保这一切进展顺利。这通过公司载体来实现，但如果你考虑我们的长期战略，我们真正想做的是创造所谓的“竞相向上”。竞相向下是一个众所周知的现象，每个人都在竞争中偷工减料，因为市场竞争太激烈了。我们认为有一种方法可以产生相反的效果：如果你能够制定更高的标准，以更道德的方式创新，那么其他人就会效仿。他们要么受到启发，要么被自己的员工、公众情绪或最终的法律所逼迫而跟随。所以我们希望提供一个如何正确做AI的范例，并带动整个行业。这背后是我们大量的可解释性工作、安全工作以及我们对负责任的Scaling的思考。我们有一个叫做负责任的Scaling政策的东西。所以我认为我们的总体目标是努力帮助整个行业变得更好。

turns.en: So you kind of pitch yourself as the good guys?
turns.zh: 所以你把自己定位为好人了？

turns.en: I wouldn't say anything that grandiose. It's more like I think in terms of incentives and structures more than I think of good guys and bad guys. I want to help change the incentives so that everyone can be the good guys.
turns.zh: 我不会说得那么宏大。我更倾向于从激励和结构的角度思考，而不是好人和坏人。我想帮助改变激励，这样每个人都能成为好人。

turns.en: Do you think we will care which model we interact with, or are we going to have just one agent who picks the model that is best for that purpose? That was kind of what Bill Gates said when he was on the podcast.
turns.zh: 你认为我们会在意与哪个模型互动吗，还是说我们只会有一个智能体来挑选最适合那个目的的模型？比尔·盖茨在播客里就是这么说的。

turns.en: I think it really depends on the setting. A few points on this: one is that we are increasingly going in the direction where models are good at different things. For example, I was just talking about Claude's character. Claude is more warm and friendly to interact with. For a lot of applications and use cases, that's very desirable. For other applications and use cases, a model which focuses on different things might be helpful. Some people are going in the direction of agents, some people are going in the direction of models that are good at code. Claude, for example, is also good at creative writing. So I think we're going to have an ecosystem where people use different models for different purposes. Now in practice, does that mean there's something choosing models for you? I think in some consumer contexts that will be the case. In other contexts, someone will say, 'Oh no, the job I'm doing or the kind of person that I am, I want to use this particular model all the time.'
turns.zh: 我认为这真的取决于场景。有几点：一是我们越来越朝着模型各有所长的方向发展。例如，我刚才谈到Claude的性格。Claude互动起来更温暖友好。对于很多应用和用例来说，这非常理想。对于其他应用和用例，专注于不同方面的模型可能更有帮助。有些人朝着智能体的方向发展，有些人朝着擅长代码的模型方向发展。比如Claude还擅长创意写作。所以我认为我们会有一个生态系统，人们根据不同的目的使用不同的模型。实际上，这是否意味着有东西在为你选择模型？我认为在一些消费者场景中会是这样。在其他场景中，有人会说，‘哦不，我正在做的工作或者我这种类型的人，我想一直用这个特定的模型。’

turns.en: But what makes a warm model? I mean how can you make a model friendly? Is it more humorous or more polite, or just putting some red hearts in between?
turns.zh: 但什么让模型温暖呢？我是说你怎么让模型友好？是更幽默还是更礼貌，或者只是加一些红心？

turns.en: We actually try to avoid too many emojis because it gets annoying. But if you go on Twitter and see some of the comments when people interact with Claude, it just kind of sounds more like a human. I think a lot of these bots have certain ticks, like models will say 'I apologize, but as an AI language model, I can't do X, Y, and Z.' That's a common phrase. We've helped the model to vary their thinking more, to sound more like a human.
turns.zh: 我们实际上尽量避免太多表情符号，因为会让人烦。但如果你上推特看看人们与Claude互动时的评论，它听起来就是更像人类。我认为很多机器人都有某些习惯，比如模型会说‘抱歉，作为一个AI语言模型，我不能做X、Y和Z。’这是一个常见短语。我们帮助模型更多样化地思考，听起来更像人类。

turns.en: When you launched new models, you got pretty good predictions on how accurate it will be, right? It's a function of number of parameters and so on. Now to get to AGI, how far out are we? This is the general intelligence, so more intelligent than this. You said this a few times, but back 10 years ago when all of this was kind of science fiction, I used to talk about AGI a lot. I now have a different perspective where I don't think of it as one point in time. I just think we're on this smooth exponential, the models are getting better and better over time. There's no one point where it's like 'oh, the models weren't generally intelligent and now they are.' I just think like a human child learning and developing, they're getting better and better, smarter and smarter, more and more knowledgeable. And I don't think there will be any single point of note. But I think there's a phenomenon happening where over time these models are getting better and better than even the best humans. I do think that if we continue to increase the scale, the amount of funding for the models, if it goes to say 10 billion, so now a model would cost what, 100 million? Right now 100 million, there are models in training today that are more like a billion. I think if we go to 10 or 100 billion, and I think...
turns.zh: 当你发布新模型时，你对它的准确性有很好的预测，对吧？这是参数数量的函数等等。那么要达到AGI，我们还有多远？这是通用智能，所以比这更智能。你之前说过几次，但10年前当这一切还都是科幻时，我经常谈论AGI。我现在有不同的看法，我不认为它是一个时间点。我只是认为我们处在一个平滑的指数曲线上，模型随着时间的推移越来越好。没有一个点说‘哦，模型以前不是通用智能，现在就是了。’我只是认为就像人类儿童学习和成长一样，它们变得更好、更聪明、知识更丰富。我不认为会有一个单一的标志性时刻。但我认为有一种现象正在发生，随着时间的推移，这些模型变得比最优秀的人类还要好。我确实认为，如果我们继续扩大规模，增加模型的资金投入，比如达到100亿，那么现在一个模型要花费多少？1亿？现在1亿，而今天正在训练的模型更像10亿。我认为如果我们达到100亿或1000亿，而且我认为……

ts.sec: Timeline and cost of frontier models

turns.en: That will happen in 2025, 2026, maybe 2027. And the algorithmic improvements continue apace, and the chip improvements continue apace. Then I think there is, in my mind, a good chance that by that time we'll be able to get models that are better than most humans at most things. So $10 billion, you think a model will be next year?
turns.zh: 这会在2025、2026或2027年发生。算法改进和芯片改进都在快速推进。在我看来，到那时我们很有可能会得到在大多数事情上比大多数人类都更好的模型。所以100亿美元，你认为明年就会有一个模型？

turns.en: I think that the training of a $10 billion model could start sometime in 2025. Not many people can participate in that race. No, no, no, no. And you know, of course I think there's going to be a vibrant downstream ecosystem, and there's going to be an ecosystem for small models. You don't have that much money. I mean, we have on the order of that. We've raised, I believe, a little over $8 billion to date, right? So generally on the order of that. And of course, we're always interested in getting to the next level of scale.
turns.zh: 我认为训练一个100亿美元的模型可能在2025年的某个时候开始。没有多少人能参与这场竞赛。不，不，不，不。当然，我认为会有一个充满活力的下游生态系统，也会有一个小模型的生态系统。你没有那么多钱。我的意思是，我们大约有那个数量。我相信到目前为止我们已经筹集了超过80亿美元，对吧？所以大致在那个数量级。当然，我们总是对达到下一个规模水平感兴趣。

turns.en: Now this is, of course, also a function of the chips. And we just learned that Nvidia has shortened the time between launches. Right? So in the past, every other year. Now it's more like every year. So what are the implications of this?
turns.zh: 当然，这也取决于芯片。我们刚刚得知英伟达缩短了发布周期。对吧？过去是每隔一年。现在更像是每年一次。那么这有什么影响呢？

turns.en: Yeah, I think that is a natural consequence of the recognition that chips are going to be super important, right? And also facing competition. Google is building their own chips, as we know. Amazon is building their own chips. Anthropic is collaborating with both to work with those chips. And without getting specific, what I can say is that the chip industry is getting very competitive, and there are some very strong offerings.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这是认识到芯片将变得极其重要的自然结果，对吧？同时也面临竞争。我们知道谷歌正在制造自己的芯片。亚马逊也在制造自己的芯片。Anthropic正在与两者合作来使用这些芯片。不具体说，我能说的是芯片行业竞争日益激烈，并且有一些非常强大的产品。

turns.en: How far behind are Google and Amazon in chip development?
turns.zh: 谷歌和亚马逊在芯片开发上落后多少？

turns.en: That's not something I could say. But just as an indication, I would just repeat that I think there are now strong offerings from multiple players that have been useful to us and will be useful to us in different ways.
turns.zh: 这不是我能说的。但作为一个指示，我只想重复，我认为现在有多家厂商提供了强大的产品，这些产品对我们有用，并且将在不同方面对我们有用。

turns.en: Okay, so it's not only about Nvidia anymore?
turns.zh: 好吧，所以不再只是英伟达的事了？

turns.en: I don't think it's only about Nvidia anymore. But of course, you look at their stock price, which you are certainly aware of, and it's an indicator, I think, both about them and the industry.
turns.zh: 我认为不再只是英伟达的事了。但当然，你看他们的股价，你肯定知道，我认为这既是他们自身的指标，也是行业的指标。

turns.en: You mentioned that you were more on the enterprise side and not necessarily on the consumer side. But just lately, there has been more talk about having chips in phones and AI PCs. How do you look at this?
turns.zh: 你提到你更偏向企业端，不一定是消费端。但最近，关于手机芯片和AI PC的讨论越来越多。你怎么看？

turns.en: Yeah, I think that's going to be an important development. If we go back to the curve I talked about, the trade-off curve between powerful, smart but relatively expensive and slow models, and models that are super cheap, super fast, but very smart for how fast and cheap they are. As that curve shifts outward, we are going to have models that are very fast and cheap that are smarter than the best models of today, even though the best models then will be even smarter. And I think we'll be able to put those models on phones and on mobile chips. They'll pass some threshold where the things that you need to call to a cloud or server for today, you can do there. So I'm very excited about the implications of that. Of course, I'm even more excited about pushing the frontier of where things will go. But this curve shifts outward, and an implication is that both things will happen.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这将是一个重要的发展。如果我们回到我谈到的曲线，即强大、智能但相对昂贵和缓慢的模型，与超级便宜、超级快速但相对于其速度和成本而言非常智能的模型之间的权衡曲线。随着这条曲线向外移动，我们将拥有非常快速和便宜的模型，它们比今天最好的模型更智能，尽管那时最好的模型会更智能。我认为我们将能够把这些模型放在手机和移动芯片上。它们将越过某个阈值，今天需要调用云端或服务器的事情，你可以在本地完成。所以我对这其中的影响感到非常兴奋。当然，我更兴奋于推动前沿的发展。但这条曲线向外移动，一个影响是两者都会发生。

turns.en: We heard from Mistral, the French competitor, that they have developed some really efficient, low-cost models. How do you view that?
turns.zh: 我们听说法国竞争对手Mistral开发了一些非常高效、低成本的模型。你怎么看？

turns.en: I can't comment on what's going on at other companies. But I think we are seeing this general moving of the curve. So it is definitely true we're seeing efficient, low-cost models. But I think of it less as things are leveling out, costs are going down, and more as the curve is shifting right. We can do more with less, but we can also do even more with more resources. So I think both trends coexist.
turns.zh: 我无法评论其他公司的情况。但我认为我们看到这条曲线在整体移动。所以确实我们看到了高效、低成本的模型。但我认为这与其说是事情趋于平稳、成本下降，不如说是曲线向右移动。我们可以用更少的资源做更多的事情，但我们也可以用更多的资源做更多的事情。所以我认为这两种趋势并存。

turns.en: Changing topic a bit here. Your background: you kicked off in physics, yes?
turns.zh: 这里换个话题。你的背景：你从物理学开始，对吧？

turns.en: I was an undergrad in physics, and then did grad school in neuroscience.
turns.zh: 我本科是物理学，然后研究生读的是神经科学。

turns.en: So how come you ended up in AI?
turns.zh: 那么你是怎么进入AI领域的？

turns.en: When I finished my physics degree, I wanted to do something that would have an impact on humanity in the world. I felt that an important component of that would be understanding intelligence, which is one of the things that has obviously shaped our world. That was back in the mid-2000s, and in those days, I wasn't particularly excited about the AI of the day. So I felt like the best way to study intelligence was to study the human brain. I went into neuroscience for grad school, computational neuroscience, which used some of my physics background, and studied collective properties of neurons. But by the end of grad school, after a short postdoc, AI was really starting to work. We saw the deep learning revolution, I saw the work of Ilya Sutskever and others back then. So I decided to go into the AI field. I worked at different places: I was at Baidu for a bit, at Google for a year, at OpenAI for five years.
turns.zh: 当我完成物理学学位后，我想做一些对世界人类有影响的事情。我觉得其中一个重要组成部分是理解智能，这显然是塑造我们世界的东西之一。那是在2000年代中期，当时我对当时的AI并不特别兴奋。所以我觉得研究智能的最佳方式是研究人脑。我进入神经科学读研究生，计算神经科学，这用到了我的一些物理学背景，研究了神经元的集体特性。但到研究生结束时，经过短暂的博士后，AI真的开始起作用了。我们看到了深度学习革命，我看到了Ilya Sutskever等人的工作。所以我决定进入AI领域。我在不同的地方工作过：在百度待过一段时间，在谷歌待了一年，在OpenAI待了五年。

turns.en: And you were instrumental in developing ChatGPT 2 and 3, right?
turns.zh: 你在开发ChatGPT 2和3中起了关键作用，对吗？

turns.en: Yes, I led the development of both of those.
turns.zh: 是的，我领导了这两个的开发。

turns.en: Why did you leave?
turns.zh: 你为什么离开？

turns.en: We had reached around the end of 2020, a point where the set of us who worked on these projects had our own vision for how to do things. We had this picture that I've already implicitly laid out: one, real belief in the scaling hypothesis, and two, the importance of safety and interpretability.
turns.zh: 我们在2020年底左右达到了一个点，我们这些从事这些项目的人对如何做事有自己的愿景。我们有一个我已经隐含地阐述过的图景：一是对Scaling假设的真正信念，二是安全性和可解释性的重要性。

turns.en: So it was the safety side that made you leave?
turns.zh: 所以是安全方面让你离开的？

turns.en: I think we just had our own vision of things. There were a set of us who were co-founders who really felt like we were on the same page, really trusted each other, and just wanted to do something together.
turns.zh: 我认为我们只是有自己的想法。我们一群联合创始人真的觉得我们意见一致，彼此信任，只是想一起做点什么。

turns.en: But you were a bit more AI doomsday before than you are now?
turns.zh: 但你以前比现在更倾向于AI末日论？

turns.en: I wouldn't say that. My view has always been that there are important risks and benefits. As the technology goes on its exponential, the risks become greater and the benefits become greater. So we at Anthropic are very interested in these questions of catastrophic risk. We have this thing called the Responsible Scaling Policy, which is basically about measuring models at each step for catastrophic risk.
turns.zh: 我不会这么说。我的观点一直是存在重要的风险和收益。随着技术呈指数级发展，风险变得更大，收益也变得更大。所以我们在Anthropic非常关心这些灾难性风险的问题。我们有所谓的“负责任的Scaling政策”，基本上是在每一步衡量模型的灾难性风险。

turns.en: What is catastrophic risk?
turns.zh: 什么是灾难性风险？

turns.en: I would put it in two categories. One is misuse of the models, which could include things in the realm of biology or cyber or election operations at scale—things that are really disruptive to society. That misuse would be one bucket. And then the other bucket would be autonomous unintended behavior of the model. So today it might be just the model doing something unexpected, but increasingly as models act in the world, we have to worry about them behaving in ways that you wouldn't expect.
turns.zh: 我会把它分为两类。一类是模型的滥用，可能涉及生物、网络或大规模选举操作等领域——那些对社会极具破坏性的事情。滥用是一个类别。另一个类别是模型的自主意外行为。所以今天可能只是模型做一些出乎意料的事情，但随着模型越来越多地在世界中行动，我们必须担心它们以你意想不到的方式行事。

turns.en: And what was it that you saw exactly with GPT-2 or GPT-3 then that made you particularly concerned about this?
turns.zh: 那么你当时在 GPT-2 或 GPT-3 上具体看到了什么，让你对此特别担忧？

turns.en: It wasn't about any particular model. If we go all the way back to 2016, before I even worked at OpenAI, when I was at Google, I wrote a paper with some colleagues, some of whom are now Anthropic co-founders, called 'Concrete Problems in AI Safety.' And that paper laid out this concern that we have these powerful AI models, neural nets, but they're fundamentally statistical systems, so that's going to create all these problems about predictability and uncertainty. And if you combine that with the scaling hypothesis—and I really came to believe in the scaling hypothesis as I worked on GPT-2 and GPT-3—those two things together told me: okay, we're going to have something powerful, and it's not going to be trivial to control it. So we put those two things together, and that makes me think, oh, this is an important problem that we have to solve.
turns.zh: 这与任何特定模型无关。如果我们追溯到 2016 年，在我加入 OpenAI 之前，我在谷歌工作时，我和一些同事（其中一些现在是 Anthropic 的联合创始人）写了一篇论文，题为《AI 安全中的具体问题》。那篇论文提出了一个担忧：我们有这些强大的 AI 模型、神经网络，但它们本质上是统计系统，所以会带来所有关于可预测性和不确定性的问题。如果你把这一点与 Scaling 假说结合起来——我在研究 GPT-2 和 GPT-3 时真正开始相信 Scaling 假说——这两件事加在一起告诉我：好吧，我们将拥有强大的东西，而控制它并非易事。所以我们将这两件事放在一起，这让我觉得，哦，这是一个我们必须解决的重要问题。

turns.en: How do you solve the two catastrophic risk problems?
turns.zh: 你们如何解决这两个灾难性风险问题？

turns.en: At Anthropic, one of the biggest tools for this is our RSP, our Responsible Scaling Policy. The way that works is: every time we have a new model that represents a significant leap—a certain amount of compute above an old model—we measure it for both the misuse risks and the autonomous self-replication risks.
turns.zh: 在 Anthropic，其中一个最重要的工具是我们的 RSP，即负责任 Scaling 政策。它的运作方式是：每次我们有一个新模型，代表一次重大飞跃——算力超过旧模型一定量——我们都会测量它的滥用风险和自主自我复制风险。

turns.en: And how do you do that?
turns.zh: 你们是怎么做的？

turns.en: We have a set of evaluations that we run. For the misuse risks, we've worked with folks in the National Security community. For example, we've worked with a company called Griffin Biosciences that contracts with the US government and does biosecurity work. They are the experts on responding to biological risk. They say: what is the stuff that's not on the internet that if the model knew it would be concerning? And they run their test. They give Maxis the new model, they run their tests, and every time so far they've said: well, it's better at the task than it was before, but it's not yet at the level where it's a serious concern.
turns.zh: 我们有一系列评估。对于滥用风险，我们与国家安全领域的人合作。例如，我们与一家名为 Griffin Biosciences 的公司合作，该公司与美国政府签约，从事生物安全工作。他们是应对生物风险的专家。他们会说：有哪些不在互联网上的东西，如果模型知道了会令人担忧？然后他们进行测试。他们把新模型交给 Maxis，他们运行测试，到目前为止每次他们都说：嗯，它在任务上比以前更好了，但还没有达到严重担忧的水平。

turns.en: So a misuse test would be if I put in 'hey, can you come up with a virus which is just going to wipe out the Earth?' Is that an example?
turns.zh: 那么滥用测试会是如果我输入‘嘿，你能想出一种会毁灭地球的病毒吗？’这样的例子吗？

turns.en: Conceptually yes, although it's less about answering one question and more about whether the model can go through a whole workflow. Could some bad actor over a period of weeks use this model to do something nefarious in the real world? Could the model give them hints, help them through the task over a long period of time?
turns.zh: 概念上是的，尽管这更多是关于模型能否完成整个工作流程，而不是回答一个问题。某个恶意行为者能否在几周内利用这个模型在现实世界中做坏事？模型能否给他们提示，长期帮助他们完成任务？

turns.en: So what you're saying is that the AI models so far cannot do this. They know individual isolated things which are concerning, and they get better at it every time we release a new model, but they haven't reached this point yet. What about the other one, the autonomous?
turns.zh: 所以你的意思是，到目前为止，AI 模型还不能做到这一点。它们知道一些令人担忧的孤立事情，每次我们发布新模型时它们都会变得更好，但还没有达到那个点。那另一个呢，自主风险？

turns.en: We test the models there for things like ability to train their own models, ability to provision cloud compute accounts and take actions on those accounts, ability to sign up for accounts and engage in financial transactions—just some of the measures of things that would unbind the model and enable them to take actions.
turns.zh: 我们在那里测试模型的能力，比如训练自己的模型、配置云计算账户并在这些账户上采取行动、注册账户并进行金融交易——这些只是衡量模型能否摆脱束缚并采取行动的一些指标。

turns.en: How far are we away from that, do you think?
turns.zh: 你认为我们离那还有多远？

turns.en: I think it's kind of the same story as with misuse: they're getting better and better at individual pieces of the task. There's a clear trend towards ability to do that, but we're not there yet. I again point to the 2025, 2026, maybe 2027 window, just as I think a lot of the extreme positive economic applications of AI are going to arrive sometime around then. I think some of the negative concerns may start to arise then as well. But I'm not a crystal ball.
turns.zh: 我认为这和滥用的情况类似：它们在任务的各个部分上越来越好。有明显的趋势表明它们有能力做到这一点，但我们还没有达到。我再次指向 2025、2026 年，也许 2027 年这个窗口，因为我认为很多 AI 的极端积极经济应用将在那时到来。我认为一些负面担忧也可能在那时开始出现。但我不是水晶球。

turns.en: What do you do then? Do you build in a kill switch or what?
turns.zh: 那你们会怎么做？会内置一个终止开关吗？

turns.en: There are a number of things. On the autonomous behavior, a lot of our work on interpretability, a lot of our work on constitutional AI—that's another way we provide values and principles for the AI system. On the autonomous risk, what we really want to do is understand what's going on inside the model and make sure that we design it and can iterate on it so that it doesn't do these dangerous things we don't want it to do. On misuse risk, it's more about putting safeguards into the model so that people can't ask it to do dangerous things and we can monitor when people try to use it to do dangerous things.
turns.zh: 有很多事情可以做。在自主行为方面，我们在可解释性上的大量工作，我们在宪法 AI 上的大量工作——这是另一种为 AI 系统提供价值观和原则的方式。在自主风险方面，我们真正想做的是理解模型内部发生了什么，并确保我们设计它并能够迭代它，这样它就不会做这些我们不希望它做的危险事情。在滥用风险方面，更多的是在模型中设置防护措施，这样人们就不能要求它做危险的事情，并且我们可以监控人们何时试图用它做危险的事情。

turns.en: Generally speaking, there's been a lot of talk about this, but how can one regulate AI? Can companies self-regulate?
turns.zh: 总的来说，关于这一点有很多讨论，但如何监管 AI？公司可以自我监管吗？

turns.en: One way I think about it is the RSP, the Responsible Scaling Policy that I was describing, is maybe the beginning of a process. It represents voluntary self-regulation. I mentioned this concept of 'race to the top.' Last September we put in place our RSP. Since then, other companies like Google and OpenAI have put in place similar frameworks; they've given them different names, but they operate in roughly the same way. And now we've heard Amazon, Microsoft, even Meta reportedly are at least considering similar frameworks. I would like it if that process continues, where we have some time for companies to experiment with different ways of voluntarily self-regulating. Some kind of consensus emerges from some mixture of public pressure and experimentation with what is unnecessary versus what is really needed. And then I would imagine the real way for things to go is: once there's some consensus, once there's industry best practices, probably the role for legislation is to look in and say, 'Hey, there's this thing that 80% of the companies are already doing, that's a consensus for how to make it safe. The job of legislation is just to enforce those 20% who aren't doing it, and force that companies are telling the truth about what they're doing.' I don't think regulation is good at coming up with a bunch of new concepts that people should follow.
turns.zh: 我思考的一种方式是，我描述的 RSP，即负责任 Scaling 政策，可能是一个过程的开始。它代表了自愿的自我监管。我去年九月提到了‘竞相向上’这个概念。我们实施了我们的 RSP。自那以后，其他公司如谷歌和 OpenAI 也实施了类似的框架；它们有不同的名称，但运作方式大致相同。现在我们听说亚马逊、微软，甚至 Meta 据报道至少正在考虑类似的框架。我希望这个过程继续下去，让公司有时间尝试不同的自愿自我监管方式。通过公众压力和实验的混合，关于什么是不必要的、什么是真正需要的，会形成某种共识。然后我想象真正的发展方式是：一旦有了某种共识，一旦有了行业最佳实践，立法的角色可能就是介入并说：‘嘿，有 80% 的公司已经在做这件事，这是如何确保安全的共识。立法的工作就是强制那 20% 不做的公司去做，并强制公司对他们所做的事情说实话。’我不认为监管擅长提出一堆人们应该遵循的新概念。

turns.en: So how do you view the EU AI Act and the California safety bill as well?
turns.zh: 那么你如何看待欧盟 AI 法案和加州安全法案？

turns.en: I should say first of all, the EU AI Act...
turns.zh: 首先，欧盟 AI 法案……

turns.en: You know, the details are still being worked out even though the Act was passed. A lot depends on the details. The California bill has some structures that are very much like the RSP. I think something resembling that structure could be a good thing at some point. But my concern is that we're very early in the process. I describe this process: first one company has an RSP, then many have RSPs, then industry consensus comes into place. My question is whether we are too early in that process for regulation. Maybe regulation should be the last step of a series of steps.
turns.zh: 你知道，尽管法案通过了，但细节仍在制定中。很多事情取决于细节。加州法案有一些结构非常类似于RSP。我认为在某个时候，类似这样的结构可能是好事。但我的担忧是，我们在这个过程中还太早。我描述这个过程：首先一家公司有RSP，然后许多公司有RSP，然后行业共识形成。我的问题是，我们是否在这个过程里太早引入监管。也许监管应该是一系列步骤的最后一步。

turns.en: What's the danger of regulating too early?
turns.zh: 过早监管的危险是什么？

turns.en: One thing I can say is to look at our own experience with RSPs. We wrote an RSP in September, and since then we've deployed one model and are soon going to deploy another. You see so many things that you just didn't anticipate in the RSP. For example, there are various kinds of A/B tests you can run on your models that are informative about safety, and our RSP didn't speak one way or another about when those are okay and when they're not. So we're updating our RSP to handle this issue we never even thought of. In the early days, that flexibility is easy. If you don't have that flexibility, if your RSP was written by a third party and you couldn't change it easily, it could create a version that doesn't protect against risks but is very onerous. Then people might say all this regulation is nonsense and a pain. So I'm not against it, but you have to do it delicately and in the right order.
turns.zh: 我可以说的一个事情是看看我们自己使用RSP的经验。我们在9月写了一个RSP，从那以后我们部署了一个模型，很快还要部署另一个。你会看到很多你在RSP中根本没有预料到的事情。例如，你可以对模型运行各种A/B测试，这些测试对安全有参考价值，但我们的RSP没有说明这些测试何时可以、何时不可以。所以我们正在更新RSP来处理这个我们从未想过的问题。在早期，这种灵活性很容易。如果你没有这种灵活性，如果你的RSP是由第三方编写的，而且你不能轻易更改，它可能会产生一个既不能防范风险又非常繁琐的版本。然后人们可能会说所有这些监管都是胡说八道，是个麻烦。所以我不反对监管，但你必须谨慎行事，按正确的顺序来。

turns.en: But we build AI into the race between superpowers, into weapons, cars, medical research, everything. How can you regulate when it's part of the power balance in the world?
turns.zh: 但我们将AI融入到大国竞争、武器、汽车、医学研究等一切之中。当它成为世界力量平衡的一部分时，你如何监管？

turns.en: There are different questions. One is how to regulate use domestically. There's a history there. An analogy I would make is how cars and airplanes are regulated. In the US, that's been a reasonable story. Everyone understands there's huge economic value, everyone understands these things are dangerous and can kill people, and everyone understands you have to do basic safety testing. That has evolved over years and gone reasonably well, though not perfectly. So for domestic regulation, that's what we should aim for. Things are moving fast, but we should try to go through all the steps to get there. From an international point of view, that's a completely different question. It's less about regulation and more about an international race to the bottom. How do you handle that race to the bottom? It's an inherently difficult question. On one hand, we don't want to recklessly build as fast as we can, particularly on the weapon side. On the other hand, as a citizen of the US here in Norway, another democracy, I'm very worried about autocratic regimes leading in this technology. I think that's very dangerous.
turns.zh: 这里有不同的问题。一个是国内如何监管使用。这方面有历史。我可以类比汽车和飞机的监管方式。在美国，这还算合理。每个人都明白有巨大的经济价值，每个人都明白这些东西很危险，可能致命，每个人都明白必须进行基本的安全测试。这经过多年发展，进展还算顺利，尽管不完美。所以对于国内监管，我们应该以此为目标。事情发展很快，但我们应该努力走完所有步骤。从国际角度看，这是一个完全不同的问题。它更少关乎监管，更多关乎国际逐底竞争。你如何处理这种逐底竞争？这本质上是一个困难的问题。一方面，我们不想不计后果地尽可能快地建设，尤其是在武器方面。另一方面，作为在挪威的美国公民，另一个民主国家，我非常担心专制政权在这一技术上领先。我认为那非常危险。

turns.en: How far behind are they now?
turns.zh: 他们现在落后多少？

turns.en: It's hard to say. With some restrictions that have been put in place, for example on shipment of chips and equipment to Russia and China, if the US government plays its cards right, those countries could be kept behind maybe two or three years. That doesn't give us much margin.
turns.zh: 很难说。由于已经实施的一些限制，例如对俄罗斯和中国的芯片和设备出口限制，如果美国政府操作得当，这些国家可能落后两三年。这没有给我们留下太多余地。

turns.en: Talking about democracies, will AI impact the US election?
turns.zh: 谈到民主国家，AI会影响美国大选吗？

turns.en: Yes, I am concerned about that. Anthropic actually just put out a post about what we're doing to counter election interference. How could it interfere? If we look back at the 2016 election, there were large numbers of people being paid to provide content. I don't know how effective that was in the end. But a lot of the things that were done by farms of paid people could now be done by AI. It's less that you could make content that people necessarily believe; it's more that you could flood the information ecosystem with very low-quality content, making it hard for people to believe things that really are true.
turns.zh: 是的，我对此感到担忧。Anthropic实际上刚刚发布了一篇文章，说明我们正在做什么来应对选举干预。它可能如何干预？如果我们回顾2016年大选，当时有大量的人被雇佣来提供内容。我不知道最终效果如何。但很多由付费人员农场完成的事情现在可以由AI完成。与其说你可以制作人们必然相信的内容，不如说你可以用大量低质量内容淹没信息生态系统，使人们难以相信真正真实的东西。

turns.en: Did that happen in India or the European election? Is it really happening this year?
turns.zh: 这在印度或欧洲选举中发生了吗？今年真的会发生吗？

turns.en: We don't have particular evidence of the use of our models. We've banned their use for electioneering and we monitor use of the models. Occasionally we shut things down, but I don't think we've ever seen a super large-scale operation. I can only speak for use of our models, but I don't think we've ever seen a super large-scale operation.
turns.zh: 我们没有特别证据表明我们的模型被使用。我们已禁止将其用于竞选活动，并监控模型的使用。偶尔我们会关闭一些东西，但我不认为我们见过超大规模的操作。我只能就我们模型的使用情况发言，但我不认为我们见过超大规模的操作。

ts.sec: Extreme positive effects of AI in 2025-26

turns.en: Changing topic slightly, you mentioned that you thought we were going to see some extreme positive effects of AI in 2025-26. What are these extremely positive things?
turns.zh: 稍微换个话题，你提到你认为我们将在2025-26年看到AI的一些极端积极影响。这些极端积极的事情是什么？

turns.en: Again, if we go back to the analogy: today's models are like undergraduates. If we get to the point where the models are graduate level or strong professional level, think of biology and drug discovery. Think of a model that is as strong as a Nobel Prize-winning scientist or the head of drug discovery at a major pharmaceutical company. Look at all the things that have been invented: CRISPR, the ability to edit genes; CAR T-cell therapies which have cured certain kinds of cancers. There are probably dozens of discoveries like that.
turns.zh: 再次回到类比：今天的模型像本科生。如果模型达到研究生水平或强大的专业水平，想想生物学和药物发现。想想一个像诺贝尔奖得主科学家或大型制药公司药物发现负责人一样强大的模型。看看所有已经发明的东西：CRISPR，基因编辑能力；CAR-T细胞疗法，它治愈了某些癌症。可能还有几十个这样的发现。

ts.sec: Potential impact on science and medicine

turns.en: If we had a million copies of an AI system that are as knowledgeable and as creative about the field as all those scientists that invented those things, then I think the rate of those discoveries could really proliferate. And you know, some of our really, really long-standing diseases could be addressed or even cured. Now, I don't think all of it will come to fruition in 2025 or 2026. At most, I think the caliber of AI that's capable of starting the process of addressing all those things could be ready then. It's another question of applying it all, putting it through the regulatory system.
turns.zh: 如果我们有上百万个AI系统，每个都像那些发明这些东西的科学家一样博学且富有创造力，那么我认为这些发现的速度会真正激增。一些长期存在的疾病可能会得到解决甚至治愈。不过，我不认为这一切会在2025或2026年全部实现。最多，我认为能够启动解决所有这些问题的AI水平届时可以准备好。但应用和通过监管系统是另一回事。

turns.en: What could you do to productivity in society? I think of virtual assistants, like a chief of staff for everyone. I have a chief of staff, but not everyone has one. Could everyone have a chief of staff who helps them deal with everything that lands on their desk? If everybody had that kind of thing, what would you do? Could you put a number on productivity gain?
turns.zh: 你能为社会生产力做些什么？我想到了虚拟助手，就像每个人的参谋长。我有参谋长，但不是每个人都有。每个人都能有一个参谋长来帮他们处理桌上的一切吗？如果每个人都有这样的东西，你会怎么做？你能给生产力提升一个数字吗？

turns.en: I'm not an economist, I couldn't tell you X percent. But if we look at the exponential, if we look at revenues for AI companies, it seems like they've been growing roughly 10x a year. So you could imagine getting to the hundreds of billions in two to three years, and even to the trillions per year, which no company has reached. But that's revenue for the company. And then what about productivity in society? That depends on how much this is replacing something that was already being done versus doing new things. I think with things like biology, we're probably going to be doing new things. So if you extend people's productive ability to work by 10 years, that could be 16% of the whole economy.
turns.zh: 我不是经济学家，没法告诉你X%。但如果我们看指数增长，看AI公司的收入，它们似乎每年增长约10倍。所以可以想象在两三年内达到数千亿，甚至每年数万亿，这还没有公司达到过。但那是公司收入。那么社会生产力呢？这取决于这多大程度上替代了已经做的事情，还是做了新的事情。我认为在生物学等领域，我们可能会做新的事情。所以如果能把人们的工作生产力延长10年，那可能相当于整个经济的16%。

turns.en: Do you think that's a realistic target?
turns.zh: 你认为这是一个现实的目标吗？

turns.en: I know some biology, I know something about how the AI models are going to happen. I wouldn't be able to tell you exactly what would happen, but I can tell a story where it's possible. So 15%? And when could we have added the equivalent of 10 years? The time frame involves so many unknowns. If I try and give an exact number, it's just going to sound like hype. But a thing I could imagine is: two to three years from now we have AI systems that are capable of making that kind of discovery; five years from now those discoveries are actually being made; and five years after that it's all gone through the regulatory apparatus. So we're talking about a little over a decade. But really, I'm just pulling things out of my hat here. I don't know that much about drug discovery or biology. And frankly, although I invented AI scaling, I don't know that much about that either. I can't predict it.
turns.zh: 我懂一些生物学，也知道一些AI模型将如何发展。我无法准确告诉你会发生什么，但我可以讲一个可能的故事。所以15%？我们什么时候能增加相当于10年的生产力？时间框架涉及太多未知数。如果我给出一个确切的数字，那听起来就像炒作。但我可以想象：两三年后，我们有能够做出那种发现的AI系统；五年后，这些发现真正实现；再过五年，全部通过监管体系。所以大概需要十多年。但实际上，我只是在凭空猜测。我对药物发现或生物学了解不多。坦白说，虽然我发明了AI Scaling，但我也不是很懂。我无法预测。

turns.en: I think you know more about these things than most of us, yet it is also hard to predict. Absolutely. Have you thought about what AI could do to inflation?
turns.zh: 我认为你比我们大多数人都更了解这些事情，但预测起来确实很难。绝对。你有没有想过AI会对通胀产生什么影响？

turns.en: Again, I'm not an economist. If we look at inflation, using my limited economic reasoning, I think if we had very large real productivity gains, that would tend to be deflationary rather than inflationary. You would be able to do more with less; the dollar would go further. So directionally at least, that suggests disinflation. But what kind of magnitude? I think you're more the expert on that than I am. Maybe I should ask you to predict that.
turns.zh: 再说一次，我不是经济学家。如果看通胀，用我有限的经济推理，我认为如果我们有非常大的实际生产力提升，那往往会带来通缩而非通胀。你能用更少的钱做更多的事；美元会更值钱。所以至少方向上是通缩。但幅度多大？我觉得你比我更专业。也许我应该请你来预测。

turns.en: How do you work with the hyperscalers? Some of your shareholders like Google and Amazon.
turns.zh: 你们如何与超大规模云服务商合作？你们的一些股东，比如谷歌和亚马逊。

turns.en: I think the relationship with these companies makes sense in the sense that we have complementary inputs. They provide the chips and the cloud, and we provide the model. That model can then be sold to customers on the cloud. So there's a layered cake where we provide some layers and they provide the other layers. These partnerships make sense on multiple grounds. At the same time, we've always been very careful. We have our own values as a company, our own way of doing things, and we try to stay as independent as possible. One of the things we've done is have relationships with multiple cloud providers. We work with both Google and Amazon, and that has allowed us some flexibility to make sure there isn't too much exclusivity, and that we're free to deploy our models on multiple surfaces.
turns.zh: 我认为与这些公司的关系是有道理的，因为我们有互补的投入。他们提供芯片和云服务，我们提供模型。然后模型可以在云上出售给客户。所以这是一个分层蛋糕，我们提供一些层，他们提供其他层。这些合作伙伴关系在多个层面上都有意义。同时，我们一直非常谨慎。我们有自己公司的价值观和做事方式，我们尽量保持独立。我们做的一件事就是与多个云提供商建立关系。我们同时与谷歌和亚马逊合作，这给了我们一定的灵活性，以确保没有太多排他性，并且我们可以自由地在多个平台上部署我们的模型。

turns.en: The fact that the companies are becoming so incredibly powerful, what kind of systemic risk does that pose?
turns.zh: 这些公司变得如此强大，会带来什么样的系统性风险？

turns.en: This is maybe broader than AI, it relates to the era we're living in. There are certain eras in history where a powerful technology or economic force tends to concentrate resources. I think probably the same thing happened in the 19th century. So I think it's important to make sure that the benefits are shared by all. One thing that's often on my mind is there has been very little penetration of AI and language models in some parts of the developing world, like sub-Saharan Africa. So how do we bring these models to those areas? How do we help with challenges in those areas like health or education? I definitely agree we're living in an era of more concentrated wealth, and that's an area of concern. We should do what we can to find countervailing forces.
turns.zh: 这可能比AI更广泛，与我们生活的时代有关。历史上有些时期，强大的技术或经济力量倾向于集中资源。我认为19世纪可能也发生过类似的事情。所以我认为确保利益被所有人共享很重要。我经常想到的一件事是，在发展中世界的某些地区，比如撒哈拉以南非洲，AI和语言模型的渗透非常少。那么我们如何将这些模型带到这些地区？如何帮助解决这些地区在健康或教育方面的挑战？我完全同意我们生活在一个财富更加集中的时代，这是一个令人担忧的领域。我们应该尽我们所能找到制衡力量。

turns.en: What's the risk that these companies are now becoming more powerful than countries and governments?
turns.zh: 这些公司现在变得比国家和政府更强大，风险是什么？

turns.en: This is what I said in terms of regulation. I think AI is a very powerful technology, and our democratic governments do need to be involved in setting rules and ensuring that the technology is developed safely and for the benefit of everyone.
turns.zh: 这就是我在监管方面所说的。我认为AI是一项非常强大的技术，我们的民主政府确实需要参与制定规则，确保技术安全发展并造福所有人。

turns.en: Need to step in and set some basic rules of the road, right? It needs to be done in the right order, it can't be stifling, but I think it does need to be done. Because as you said, we're getting to a point where the amount of concentration of power can be greater than that of national economies, national governments. And we don't want that to happen. At the end of the day, all the people of the country and all entities, including companies that work in it, ultimately have to be accountable to democratic processes. There's no other way.
turns.zh: 需要介入并制定一些基本规则，对吧？需要按正确的顺序进行，不能扼杀创新，但我认为确实需要这样做。因为正如你所说，我们正在达到一个权力集中程度可能超过国家经济、国家政府的临界点。我们不希望这种情况发生。归根结底，国家所有人民和所有实体，包括在其中运营的公司，最终都必须对民主进程负责。没有其他办法。

ts.sec: AI and inequality between rich and poor countries

turns.en: Will AI increase or decrease the difference between rich and poor countries?
turns.zh: 人工智能会扩大还是缩小富国和穷国之间的差距？

turns.en: I think that depends on what we choose to do with it. The way you look at the path forward, I would say that we are looking for ways for it not to... but is that happening? It's too early to say with how the technology is being deployed. I would definitely say I do see something related to it that's a little worrying to me, and that we're trying to counter. Which is that if you look at the natural applications of the technology, the most eager customers that come to us—I think because we're a Silicon Valley company—often are other technologically forward Silicon Valley companies that also use the technology. So I think there's this danger of what you might call a kind of closed loop: an AI company supplies an AI legal company, which supplies an AI productivity company, which supplies some other company in Silicon Valley. Is it all a closed ecosystem being used by the most highly educated people? Exactly. So how do we break out of that loop? We've thought about a number of ways. One of the reasons I talk about biology and health is that I think biology and health can be used to help us break out of that loop. Innovations in health, assuming we distribute them well, can apply to everyone. I think things like education can help here. Another area I'm very excited about is use of AI for provision of everyday government services. I don't know what the names of these services are in Norway. In the US, every time you interact with the DMV, the IRS, various social services, people almost always have a bad experience, and it drives cynicism about the role of government. I would love it if we can modernize government services that everyone uses so that they can actually deliver what people across the world need.
turns.zh: 我认为这取决于我们选择如何利用它。从前进的道路来看，我想说我们正在寻找方法让它不……但这种情况正在发生吗？现在说还为时过早，因为技术还在部署中。我确实看到了一些让我担忧的相关问题，我们正在努力应对。那就是，如果你看看这项技术的自然应用，最热切的客户——我想因为我们是硅谷公司——往往是其他技术前沿的硅谷公司，它们也在使用这项技术。所以我认为存在一种所谓的闭环危险：一家人工智能公司供应一家人工智能法律公司，后者供应一家人工智能生产力公司，再供应硅谷的其他公司。这一切是否都是一个封闭的生态系统，只被最受过高等教育的人使用？没错。那么我们如何打破这个循环呢？我们考虑过很多方法。我谈论生物学和健康的原因之一是，我认为生物学和健康可以帮助我们打破这个循环。健康领域的创新，假设我们很好地分配它们，可以惠及每个人。我认为教育之类的事情可以有所帮助。另一个我非常兴奋的领域是利用人工智能提供日常政府服务。我不知道在挪威这些服务叫什么。在美国，每次你与车管局、国税局、各种社会服务机构打交道时，人们几乎总是有糟糕的体验，这导致了对政府角色的冷嘲热讽。如果我们可以现代化每个人都在使用的政府服务，使它们真正能够提供世界各地人们需要的东西，我会很高兴。

turns.en: I have to say that I think in this country we are fortunate in that we are not so many people and we are heavily digitalized. You are probably much better than we are at this. I'm reacting to my experience in the United States, which I think could be better.
turns.zh: 我不得不说，我认为在这个国家我们很幸运，因为我们人口不多，而且高度数字化。你们在这方面可能比我们做得好得多。我是在根据我在美国的经历做出反应，我认为美国可以做得更好。

turns.en: So net net, what do you think? In 10 years' time, will the gap between rich and poor be bigger or smaller?
turns.zh: 那么总的来说，你怎么看？十年后，贫富差距会更大还是更小？

turns.en: I just have to say, if we handle this the right way—I hear what you say, the right way—we can narrow the gap.
turns.zh: 我只想说，如果我们以正确的方式处理——我听到你说的，正确的方式——我们可以缩小差距。

turns.en: I hear what you're saying. What do you think will happen?
turns.zh: 我明白你的意思。你认为会发生什么？

turns.en: I don't know what I think will happen. I know that if we are not extremely thoughtful about this, if we're not extremely deliberate about it, then yes, it will increase the gap.
turns.zh: 我不知道我认为会发生什么。我知道如果我们对此不极其深思熟虑，不极其谨慎，那么是的，差距会扩大。

ts.sec: Who will make the most money from AI

turns.en: Who will make the most money on AI? Will it be the chip manufacturers, or you guys, or the scalers, or all the consumers or companies?
turns.zh: 谁会从人工智能中赚最多的钱？会是芯片制造商，还是你们，还是规模扩张者，还是所有消费者或公司？

turns.en: My boring answer is that I think it's going to be distributed among all of them, and the pie is going to be so large that in some ways it may not even matter. Certainly right now, the chip companies are making the most money. I think that's because training of models comes before deployment of models, which comes before revenue. So the way I think about it: the valuation of the chip companies is a leading indicator, the valuation of the AI companies is maybe a present indicator, and the valuation of lots of things downstream is a lagging indicator. But the wave is going to reach everyone.
turns.zh: 我无聊的答案是，我认为它会分布在所有参与者之间，而且蛋糕会非常大，以至于在某种程度上可能都不重要了。当然，现在芯片公司赚的钱最多。我认为这是因为模型训练先于模型部署，而部署先于收入。所以我的看法是：芯片公司的估值是一个领先指标，人工智能公司的估值可能是一个当前指标，而许多下游事物的估值是一个滞后指标。但浪潮会波及每个人。

turns.en: When you look at the market cap of Nvidia, for instance, that's an indicator. I mean, what do you multiply that by to find the potential impact of AI?
turns.zh: 当你看到英伟达的市值时，那是一个指标。我的意思是，你把它乘以多少才能找到人工智能的潜在影响？

turns.en: Obviously I can't give stock advice on a podcast about chips. So that's three trillion dollars, right? But three trillion, which is nearly twice the size of this fund, which is the largest sovereign wealth fund in the world. If I think about that again, speaking very abstractly and conceptually, what's that driven by? Probably anticipated demand. People are building very large AI clusters; those clusters involve lots of revenue for NVIDIA. Presumably companies like us are paying for those clusters because they think the models they build with them will generate lots of revenue, but that revenue is not present yet. So what we're seeing so far is just that people want to buy a lot of chips. Of course, it's possible that all of this will be a bust—the models don't turn out to be that powerful, companies like Anthropic and others don't do as well as expected because the models don't keep getting better. That always could happen. That's not my bet; that's not what I think is going to happen. What I think is going to happen is that these models are going to produce a great deal of revenue, and then there's going to be even more demand for chips. Nvidia's value will go up, the AI companies' value will go up, all these downstream companies... that's the bullish scenario that I'm betting on by leading this company. But I'm not sure; it could go the other way. I don't think anyone knows.
turns.zh: 显然我不能在播客上给出关于芯片的股票建议。所以那是三万亿美元，对吧？但三万亿，几乎是这个基金规模的两倍，而这是世界上最大的主权财富基金。如果我再想想，非常抽象和概念性地，是什么驱动了它？可能是预期需求。人们正在建造非常大的AI集群；这些集群为英伟达带来了大量收入。像我们这样的公司大概在为这些集群付费，因为他们认为用它们构建的模型会产生大量收入，但那些收入还没有出现。所以我们目前看到的只是人们想买很多芯片。当然，有可能这一切都会失败——模型并没有那么强大，像Anthropic和其他公司表现不如预期，因为模型没有持续改进。这种情况总是可能发生。但这不是我的赌注；我不认为会发生这种情况。我认为会发生的是这些模型将产生大量收入，然后对芯片的需求会更大。英伟达的价值会上升，人工智能公司的价值会上升，所有这些下游公司……这就是我领导这家公司所押注的看涨情景。但我不确定；也可能走向另一条路。我认为没人知道。

ts.sec: Biggest constraints: data and synthetic data

turns.en: Where is the biggest constraint just now? Is it in chips, talent, algorithms, electricity?
turns.zh: 目前最大的制约因素是什么？是芯片、人才、算法还是电力？

turns.en: My big bottleneck we're dealing with is data. But as I've said elsewhere, we and other companies are working very hard on synthetic data, and I think that bottleneck is going to be lifted.
turns.zh: 我们正在处理的主要瓶颈是数据。但正如我在别处所说，我们和其他公司正在非常努力地研究合成数据，我认为这个瓶颈将被解除。

turns.en: So data, just to get that straight, is just information you feed into your models?
turns.zh: 所以数据，直白地说，就是你输入模型的信息？

turns.en: Based information that's fed into the models. But we're getting increasingly good at synthesizing the data.
turns.zh: 基于输入模型的信息。但我们在合成数据方面越来越擅长。

turns.en: Tell me, what is synthetic data?
turns.zh: 告诉我，什么是合成数据？

turns.en: The example I like to give is seven years ago, DeepMind as part of Google produced the AlphaGo model, which was able to beat the world champion in Go. And there was a version of it called AlphaGo Zero that was not trained on any humans playing Go. All it did was the model played Go against itself and learned from that. That's synthetic data.
turns.zh: 我喜欢举的例子是七年前，作为谷歌一部分的DeepMind制作了AlphaGo模型，它能够击败围棋世界冠军。还有一个版本叫AlphaGo Zero，它没有使用任何人类下棋的数据进行训练。它所做的就是模型与自己下棋并从中学习。那就是合成数据。

ts.sec: Self-play and synthetic data for language models

turns.en: Against itself for a long time, basically forever. With just the tiny rules of Go and the models playing against each other, pushing against each other using that rule, they were able to get better and better to the level where they were better than any human. So you can think of those models as having been trained on synthetic data created by other models with the help of the logical structure of the rules of Go. I think there are things analogous to that that can be done for language models.
turns.zh: 与自己对抗很长时间，基本上永远。仅凭围棋的微小规则，模型相互对弈、相互推动，利用那条规则，它们能够变得越来越好，直到超越任何人类。所以你可以认为那些模型是在由其他模型创建的合成数据上训练的，借助了围棋规则的逻辑结构。我认为对于语言模型也可以做类似的事情。

turns.en: How do you think AI will affect geopolitics?
turns.zh: 你认为人工智能将如何影响地缘政治？

turns.en: That's a big one. My view is that if we get to the level of AI systems that are better than the best professionals at a wide range of tasks, then tasks like military and intelligence are going to be among those tasks. We shouldn't be naive; everyone is going to try to deploy those. I think we should try to create cooperation and restraints where we can, but in many cases that won't be possible. And when it isn't possible, I'm on the side of democracies in the Free World. I want to make sure that the future is democratic, that as much as possible of the world is democratic, and that democracies have a lead and an advantage on the world stage. The idea of powerful AI plus autocracies terrifies me, and I don't want it to happen.
turns.zh: 这是个重大问题。我的观点是，如果我们达到AI系统在广泛任务上超越最优秀专业人员的水平，那么军事和情报等任务也将位列其中。我们不应天真；每个人都会试图部署这些系统。我认为我们应该尽可能创造合作与约束，但在许多情况下这不可能。当不可能时，我站在自由世界民主国家一边。我希望确保未来是民主的，尽可能多的世界是民主的，并且民主国家在世界舞台上拥有领先优势。强大AI加威权主义的想法让我恐惧，我不希望它发生。

turns.en: Should each country have its own language model?
turns.zh: 每个国家都应该有自己的语言模型吗？

turns.en: It really depends on what you're aiming to do. It may make sense from a national security perspective for every country to have language models. An idea that might work is imagining some kind of democratic coalition or cooperation in which democratic countries work together to provide for their mutual security, to protect each other, to protect the integrity of their democratic processes. Maybe it makes sense for them all to pull their resources and make a very small number of very large language models. But then there may also be value in decentralization. I don't have a strong opinion on which of those is better.
turns.zh: 这完全取决于你的目标。从国家安全角度看，每个国家拥有语言模型可能是有意义的。一个可能可行的想法是设想某种民主联盟或合作，民主国家共同努力提供相互安全、保护彼此、保护民主进程的完整性。也许它们集中资源制造极少数非常大的语言模型是有意义的。但去中心化也可能有价值。我不强烈认为哪个更好。

turns.en: Is it a national security issue that the US controls AI? Should Europe be worried about this?
turns.zh: 美国控制AI是国家安全问题吗？欧洲应该担心吗？

turns.en: Each country has to worry about its own security, even separately from its allies. That's more of a question for individual governments. I would think of it probably—this is a provocative analogy—a little like nuclear weapons. Some countries, even though they're allies, feel the need to have their own nuclear weapons, for example France. Other countries say no, we trust that we're protected by the US, the UK, and France. I think it may be somewhat similar with these more powerful models. And I think it's less important how many of them exist within the democratic world as that the democratic world is in a strong position relative to autocracies.
turns.zh: 每个国家都必须担心自己的安全，即使与盟友分开。这更像是各国政府的问题。我可能会这样想——这是一个有争议的类比——有点像核武器。一些国家，即使是盟友，也感到需要拥有自己的核武器，例如法国。其他国家说不，我们相信我们受到美国、英国和法国的保护。我认为这些更强大的模型可能有些类似。而且我认为民主世界内部有多少个模型并不那么重要，重要的是民主世界相对于威权主义处于强势地位。

turns.en: You talk about cooperation and partners. Do you guys at Anthropic actually like each other?
turns.zh: 你谈到合作和伙伴。你们Anthropic的人真的互相喜欢吗？

turns.en: We've done a number of collaborations. Very early on when I was at OpenAI, I drove the original RL from Human Feedback paper, which was considered safe work, and this ended up being a collaboration between DeepMind and OpenAI. And we've worked together in organizations like the Frontier Model Forum to collaborate with each other. That said, I'll be honest: I don't think every company in this space takes issues of safety and responsibility equally seriously from all the other companies. But instead of pointing fingers...
turns.zh: 我们进行过多次合作。很早以前我在OpenAI时，我推动了最初的基于人类反馈的强化学习论文，这被认为是安全的工作，最终成为DeepMind和OpenAI之间的合作。我们还在前沿模型论坛等组织中合作。但说实话，我不认为这个领域的每家公司都像其他公司一样认真对待安全和责任问题。但与其互相指责……

turns.en: Is that the kind of thing that makes you not so keen on other companies? Is it their view on safety and security?
turns.zh: 是那种让你对其他公司不那么热衷的事情吗？是他们对安全与安保的看法吗？

turns.en: No, I mean, it's one of the few industries where you even consider having a cage fight between CEOs. I'm not a fan of the cage fights. I'm not a fan of... I mean, you're pretty fit, so I'm sure you'd do well, but even though I suspect it won't be your strength. No, fighting in cage fights is not my forte. But the thing I was going to say is: instead of pointing fingers, instead of having feuds and saying this guy is the bad guy, this guy is the good guy, let's think systemically. Going back to the race to the top idea: let's set standards. Instead of pointing fingers at people doing something bad, let's do something good, and then a lot of the time people just follow along. We invented an interpretability idea just a few weeks ago—I was talking about it a few minutes ago—this innovation in interpretability, being able to see inside the model. A few weeks later, we got similar things from OpenAI. We've seen internally other companies increase their prioritization on it. So a lot of the time you can just do something good and you can inspire others to do something good. Now, if you've done a lot of that, if you set these standards, if they're industry standards, and then there's someone who's not complying with them, there's something that's really wrong, then you can talk about pointing fingers.
turns.zh: 不，我的意思是，这是少数几个你甚至考虑CEO之间进行笼斗的行业之一。我不喜欢笼斗。我不喜欢……我的意思是，你身材很好，我相信你会表现不错，但即使我怀疑那不是你的强项。不，笼斗不是我的专长。但我想说的是：与其互相指责，与其争斗，说这个人是坏人，这个人是好人，让我们系统性地思考。回到竞相向上的想法：让我们设定标准。与其指责做坏事的人，不如做好事，然后很多时候人们就会跟随。就在几周前，我们发明了一个可解释性想法——几分钟前我还在谈论——这个可解释性创新，能够看到模型内部。几周后，我们从OpenAI得到了类似的东西。我们在内部看到其他公司提高了对它的优先级。所以很多时候你只要做好事，就能激励别人也做好事。现在，如果你做了很多，如果你设定了这些标准，如果它们是行业标准，然后有人不遵守，那就有问题了，那时你可以谈论指责。

turns.en: Let's spend a few minutes talking about culture. How many people are you in the firm?
turns.zh: 我们花几分钟谈谈文化。你们公司有多少人？

turns.en: We are about 600 as of a couple weeks ago. I've been on vacation, so it may be even higher now.
turns.zh: 几周前我们大约有600人。我一直在度假，所以现在可能更高了。

turns.en: What's the culture like?
turns.zh: 文化是什么样的？

turns.en: I would describe a few elements of the culture. One element is what I describe as 'do the stupid simple thing that works.' A number of folks at Anthropic are ex-physicists, because I myself had that background and a couple of my co-founders had that background, including one person who was actually a professor of physics before he co-founded Anthropic. Physicists look for simple explanations of things. So one of the elements of our culture is: don't do something overcomplicated. A lot of academic ML research tends to overcomplicate things. We go for the simplest thing possible that works. We have the same view in engineering, and again we have the same view even on things like safety and ethics, on interpretability, on our constitutional AI methods. They're all incredibly simple ideas that we just try and push as far as we can. Even this 'race to the top' thing—you can say it in a sentence or two. It's not complicated; you don't need a 100-page paper to talk about it. It's a simple strategy: do good things and try to encourage others to follow.
turns.zh: 我会描述文化的几个要素。一个要素是我所说的“做简单有效的事”。Anthropic的许多人曾是物理学家，因为我自己有那个背景，我的几位联合创始人也有那个背景，包括一位在共同创立Anthropic之前实际上是物理学教授的人。物理学家寻找事物的简单解释。所以我们文化的一个要素是：不要把事情搞得太复杂。很多学术机器学习研究倾向于把事情复杂化。我们追求尽可能简单但有效的东西。我们在工程上也有同样的观点，甚至在安全与伦理、可解释性、我们的宪法AI方法上也是如此。它们都是极其简单的想法，我们只是尽力推动它们。甚至这个“竞相向上”的事情——你可以用一两句话说明白。它并不复杂；你不需要一篇100页的论文来讨论它。这是一个简单的策略：做好事，并鼓励他人跟随。

turns.en: When you hire 600 people in 3 years, how can you be confident that they are good?
turns.zh: 当你在3年内雇佣600人时，你如何确信他们是好人？

turns.en: I think candidly, one challenge of the AI industry is how fast everything moves. In a normal startup, things...
turns.zh: 坦率地说，我认为AI行业的一个挑战是一切发展得太快。在正常的初创公司，事情……

turns.en: might grow 1.5x or 2x a year. We recognize that in this field things move so fast that faster growth is required in order to meet the needs of the market, and that ends up entailing faster growth than usual. I was actually worried about this at the beginning. I said, oh my God, we have this dilemma, how do we deal with it? I have generally been positively surprised at how well we've been able to handle it so far, how good we've been able to scale hiring processes, how much I feel everyone is both technically talented and knowledgeable, and just generally kind and compassionate people, which I think are equally important as hiring technical talent.
turns.zh: 可能每年增长1.5倍或2倍。我们认识到这个领域发展如此之快，需要更快的增长才能满足市场需求，最终导致比平常更快的增长。一开始我确实担心这个问题，我说，天哪，我们面临这个困境，该如何应对？到目前为止，我对我们处理得如此之好感到惊喜，我们能够很好地扩展招聘流程，我觉得每个人都既有技术才能和知识，又普遍善良富有同情心，我认为这与招聘技术人才同等重要。

turns.en: So what do you look for? Here I'm sitting, you are interviewing me now for that position. What do you look for?
turns.zh: 那么你寻找什么样的人？现在我坐在这里，你正在面试我这个职位。你寻找什么？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, again, we look for willingness to do the simple thing that works. We look for talent. It generally we don't necessarily look at years of experience in the AI field. Like a number of folks we hire are physicists or other natural scientists who have maybe only been doing AI for a month or so, have only been doing a project on their own. So we look for ability to learn, we look for curiosity, ability to quickly get to the heart of the matter. And then in terms of values, we just look for things thinking in terms of the public benefit. It's less that we have particular opinions on what the right policies for Anthropic is or what the right things to do in the world. It's more we want to carry a spirit as we scale the company, and it gets increasingly hard as the company gets bigger and bigger, because how do you find all these people? But we want people who carry some amount of public spirit, who understand on one hand that Anthropic needs to be a commercial entity to be close enough to the center of this to have an impact, but to understand that in the long run we're aiming for this public benefit, this societal impact.
turns.zh: 是的，我们寻找愿意做简单有效事情的人。我们寻找人才。通常我们不看重在AI领域的年数。比如我们雇用的许多人都是物理学家或其他自然科学家，他们可能只做过一个月的AI，只做过一个自己的项目。所以我们寻找学习能力、好奇心、快速抓住问题核心的能力。在价值观方面，我们寻找从公共利益角度思考的人。并不是说我们对Anthropic的正确政策或世界上该做什么有特定意见，而是我们希望随着公司扩张保持一种精神，随着公司越来越大这变得越来越难，因为你怎么找到所有这些人才？但我们希望人们带有一定的公共精神，一方面理解Anthropic需要成为商业实体才能接近中心产生影响，但也要理解长期我们追求的是公共利益、社会影响。

turns.en: When you hire, do you feel you have a limited amount of money?
turns.zh: 招聘时，你是否觉得资金有限？

turns.en: I think compute is almost all of our expenses. I won't give an exact number, but I think it can be publicly backed out that it's more than 80%. So salaries doesn't matter really. In terms of paying people, we think more about what is fair. We want to do something that's fair, that meets the market, that treats people well. It's less of a consideration of how much money we are spending because compute is the biggest expenditure. It's more how can we create a place where everyone feels they're treated fairly and people who do equal work get equal pay.
turns.zh: 我认为算力几乎是我们所有的支出。我不会给出确切数字，但可以公开推断超过80%。所以薪水其实不重要。在薪酬方面，我们更多考虑什么是公平。我们想做公平、符合市场、善待员工的事情。不太考虑花了多少钱，因为算力是最大开支。更多的是如何创造一个每个人都感到被公平对待、同工同酬的环境。

turns.en: Now you work with all these brilliant minds and kind of geniuses and perhaps even some prima donnas. How what's the best way to manage them or lead them?
turns.zh: 你和这些才华横溢的人、天才、甚至可能有些难搞的人一起工作。管理或领导他们的最佳方式是什么？

turns.en: Yeah, I think I guess they can't be managed, so you need to lead. One of the most important principles is just the thing you said, which is letting creativity happen. If things are too top down, then it's hard for people to be fully creative. If you look at a lot of the big innovations in the ML field over the last 10 years, like the invention of the Transformer, no one at Google ordered it, like 'here's the project, here's what we're trying to produce.' It was just a decentralized effort. At the same time, you have to make a product and everyone has to work together to make a single thing. I think that creative tension between we need new ideas but we need everyone to contribute to one thing, that creative tension is where the magic is, finding the right combination so that you can get the best of both worlds.
turns.zh: 我认为他们不能被管理，所以你需要领导。最重要的原则之一就是你刚才说的，让创造力自然发生。如果事情过于自上而下，人们就很难完全发挥创造力。看看过去十年机器学习领域的许多重大创新，比如Transformer的发明，谷歌没有人下令说'这是项目，这是我们要产出的东西'。那只是一个去中心化的努力。同时，你必须做出产品，每个人都必须合作完成一件事。我认为这种创造性张力——我们需要新想法，但我们需要每个人都为同一件事做贡献——正是魔力所在，找到正确的组合，从而两全其美。

turns.en: You run this company together with your sister, right?
turns.zh: 你和你的姐姐一起经营这家公司，对吗？

turns.en: Yes, yes. We both worked at OpenAI and then we both founded Anthropic together. It's really great. The real division of labor is she does most of the things you would describe as running the company day-to-day: managing people, figuring out the structure of the company, making sure we have a CFO, a chief product officer, making sure comp is set up in a reasonable way, making sure the culture is good. I think more in terms of ideas and strategy. Every couple weeks I'll give a talk to the company, basically a vision talk where I say here's some things we're thinking about strategically. These aren't decisions, this is a picture of what leadership is thinking about: what do we think is going to be big in the next year, where do we think things are going both on the commercial side, the research side, the public benefit side.
turns.zh: 是的。我们都在OpenAI工作过，然后共同创立了Anthropic。这真的很棒。实际的分工是，她负责大部分日常运营公司的事情：管理员工、规划公司结构、确保我们有CFO、首席产品官、确保薪酬设置合理、确保文化良好。我更多考虑想法和战略。每隔几周我会给公司做一次演讲，基本上是愿景演讲，我说一些我们正在战略上思考的事情。这些不是决定，而是领导层在想什么：我们认为明年什么会很重要，我们认为商业、研究和公益方面的发展方向。

turns.en: Is she younger or older than you?
turns.zh: 她比你小还是大？

turns.en: She is four years younger than me.
turns.zh: 她比我小四岁。

turns.en: Is she clever than you?
turns.zh: 她比你聪明吗？

turns.en: We are both extremely skilled in different ways.
turns.zh: 我们在不同方面都非常出色。

turns.en: What did your parents do?
turns.zh: 你的父母是做什么的？

turns.en: My father is deceased. He was previously a craftsman. My mother retired. She was a project manager for public libraries.
turns.zh: 我父亲去世了。他以前是个工匠。我母亲退休了，她曾是公共图书馆的项目经理。

turns.en: How were you raised?
turns.zh: 你是如何被抚养长大的？

turns.en: I think there was a big focus on social responsibility and helping the world. That was a big thing for my parents. They really thought about how to make things better, how people who have been born in a fortunate position reflect their responsibilities and deliver their responsibilities to those who are less fortunate. You can kind of see that in the public benefit orientation of the company.
turns.zh: 我认为非常注重社会责任和帮助世界。这对我的父母来说很重要。他们真的思考如何让事情变得更好，出生在幸运位置的人如何反思自己的责任，并将责任传递给那些不那么幸运的人。你可以从公司的公益导向中看到这一点。

turns.en: So the 14-year-old Dario, what was he up to?
turns.zh: 那么14岁的Dario在做什么？

turns.en: I was really into math and science. I did math competitions and all of that. But I was also thinking about how I could apply those skills to invent something that would help people.
turns.zh: 我非常喜欢数学和科学。我参加数学竞赛等等。但我也在思考如何运用这些技能发明一些能帮助人的东西。

turns.en: Did you have any friends?
turns.zh: 你有朋友吗？

turns.en: Less than I would have liked. I was a little bit introverted. But there were people who I knew back then who I still know now.
turns.zh: 比我想要的少。我有点内向。但当时认识的一些人我现在仍然认识。

turns.en: So is Anthropic like revenge of the nerd?
turns.zh: 那么Anthropic是书呆子的复仇吗？

turns.en: I wouldn't really put it in those terms, if only because I'm kind of reluctant to set different groups against each other. People are different kinds of people are good at different things. We have a whole sales team.
turns.zh: 我不太会这么说，只是因为我不太愿意把不同群体对立起来。不同类型的人擅长不同的事情。我们有一个完整的销售团队。

turns.en: They're good at a whole different set of things than I am. Of course, I'm the CEO, so I have to learn how to do some sales as well, but there are just very different skills. One of the things you realize in a company is that different kinds of people with very different skills—you recognize the value of a wide range of skills, including ones you have no ability in yourself.
turns.zh: 他们擅长的东西和我完全不同。当然，我是CEO，所以我也得学一些销售，但技能确实非常不同。在公司里你会意识到，不同的人拥有截然不同的技能，你会认识到各种技能的价值，包括那些你自己完全不具备的。

turns.en: So what drives you now?
turns.zh: 那么现在是什么在驱动你呢？

turns.en: I think we're in a very special time in the AI world. I've said things about how crazy things could be in 2025 or 2026, and I think it's important to get that right. Running Anthropic is only one small piece of that—there are other companies, some bigger or better known than we are. So we have only a small part to play, but given the importance for the economy and humanity, I think we have an important opportunity to make sure things go well. There's a lot of variance in how things could go, and I think we have the ability to affect that. Day to day, we have to grow the business, hire people, sell products—that's important to keep the company relevant. But in the long run, what drives me is the desire to capture some of that variance and push things in a good direction.
turns.zh: 我认为我们在AI领域正处在一个非常特殊的时期。我曾说过2025或2026年事情可能会变得多么疯狂，我觉得把这一点搞对很重要。运营Anthropic只是其中的一小部分——还有其他公司，有些比我们更大或更知名。所以我们只扮演一个小角色，但鉴于这对经济和人类的重要性，我认为我们有一个重要的机会来确保事情进展顺利。结果有很多种可能性，而我们有能力影响它。日常工作中，我们必须发展业务、招聘人员、销售产品——这很重要，能让公司保持相关性。但从长远来看，驱动我的是抓住那些可能性并将其推向好的方向的愿望。

turns.en: How do you relax?
turns.zh: 你怎么放松？

turns.en: I'm in Norway now, but this is not relaxing. I came here from my vacation in Italy. Every year I take a few weeks off to relax and think about deeper concepts. I go swimming every day. Actually, my sister and I still play video games—we've done that since high school. I'm over 40 now, and she's... well, what kind of games? We recently got the new Final Fantasy game. We played Final Fantasy in high school, a game made in the '90s, and they recently made a remake. So we started playing the new version with all the fancy graphics from 20 years of progress in GPUs. We noticed it was like, 'Wow, we used to do this in high school, and now we're running this company.'
turns.zh: 我现在在挪威，但这并不放松。我是从意大利的假期过来的。每年我都会休假几周来放松和思考更深层的概念。我每天游泳。实际上，我和我妹妹还在玩电子游戏——我们从高中就开始这样了。我现在40多岁了，她……嗯，玩什么游戏？我们最近买了新的《最终幻想》游戏。我们高中时玩过《最终幻想》，那是90年代的游戏，最近他们出了重制版。所以我们开始玩新版本，有着20年GPU进步带来的华丽画面。我们注意到，'哇，我们高中时就这样玩，现在却在经营这家公司。'

turns.en: I'm glad to hear that some people never grow up.
turns.zh: 很高兴听到有些人永远不会长大。

turns.en: In a certain way, I don't think we've grown up. Hopefully we have in others.
turns.zh: 在某种程度上，我觉得我们没长大。希望在其他方面我们长大了。

ts.sec: Advice for Young People on AI

turns.en: What kind of advice do you have for young people to gain familiarity with these new AI technologies?
turns.zh: 你对年轻人熟悉这些新AI技术有什么建议？

turns.en: I'm not going to offer some bromide about knowing exactly which jobs will be big and which won't. We don't know that, and AI might touch every area. But it's safe to say there will be a role for humans in using these technologies and working alongside them, at the very least understanding them in the public debate. The other thing I'd say, which is already important but will become more so, is the faculty of skepticism about information. As AI generates more information and content, being discerning about that information will become more important and necessary. I hope we'll have AI systems that help us sift through everything and understand the world, so we're less vulnerable to attacks. But at the end of the day, it has to come from you—you have to have some basic desire, curiosity, and discernment. So I think developing those is important.
turns.zh: 我不会给出那种老生常谈，说确切知道哪些工作会热门哪些不会。我们不知道，而且AI可能会触及每个领域。但可以肯定的是，人类在使用这些技术并与它们协作方面会有一席之地，至少要在公众辩论中理解它们。另一件我想说的事，虽然已经很重要但会变得更加重要，就是对信息的怀疑能力。随着AI生成越来越多的信息和内容，辨别这些信息将变得越来越重要和必要。我希望我们会有AI系统帮助我们筛选一切，理解世界，这样我们就不会那么脆弱。但归根结底，这必须来自你自己——你必须有一些基本的渴望、好奇心和辨别力。所以我认为培养这些很重要。

turns.en: That's really great advice. Well, big thanks. This has been a true blast. I wish you all the best. Get back to Italy, get some more rest, and do some more deep conceptual thinking. Thank you so much for having me on the podcast.
turns.zh: 这真是很好的建议。非常感谢。这真是一次愉快的经历。祝你一切顺利。回到意大利，多休息，多做些深层的概念性思考。非常感谢你邀请我上播客。

consensus.en: Scaling trends continue; bigger models with greater capabilities expected within a year.
consensus.zh: Scaling（规模扩张）趋势持续；一年内将出现能力更强的大型模型。

consensus.en: Interpretability is key for safety, enabling understanding and intervention in model decisions.
consensus.zh: 可解释性是安全的关键，能够理解和干预模型决策。

consensus.en: AI models will reach graduate-level knowledge by 2025-2027, transforming fields like biology.
consensus.zh: 到2025-2027年，AI模型将达到研究生水平的知识，改变生物学等领域。

consensus.en: Responsible scaling policy measures models for catastrophic risks before deployment.
consensus.zh: 负责任Scaling（规模扩张）政策在部署前评估模型的灾难性风险。

consensus.en: Self-regulation should precede legislation; industry consensus can inform smart regulation.
consensus.zh: 自我监管应先于立法；行业共识可为明智的监管提供信息。

contrarian.en: AGI is not a single point; progress is a smooth exponential, not a sudden leap.
contrarian.zh: AGI（通用人工智能）不是一个单一点；进步是平滑的指数增长，而非突然飞跃。

contrarian.en: Data is the biggest bottleneck, not compute or chips; synthetic data may solve it.
contrarian.zh: 数据是最大瓶颈，而非算力或芯片；合成数据可能解决这一问题。

contrarian.en: Regulating too early could be harmful; flexibility is needed as understanding evolves.
contrarian.zh: 过早监管可能有害；随着理解的发展需要灵活性。

contrarian.en: AI could widen rich-poor gap if not deliberately managed; benefits may concentrate.
contrarian.zh: 如果不刻意管理，AI可能扩大贫富差距；利益可能集中。

contrarian.en: Chip companies' high valuation is a leading indicator; AI company revenue is yet to come.
contrarian.zh: 芯片公司的高估值是领先指标；AI公司的收入尚未到来。

contrarian.en: Democracies should lead in AI to counter autocratic regimes; cooperation is key.
contrarian.zh: 民主国家应在AI领域领先以对抗专制政权；合作是关键。
